findings.reachability: Any logged-in WordPress user (wp_ajax_ requires authentication by default, but no capability check is performed beyond being logged in)
findings.justification: The wp_ajax_plt_dismiss_notice handler calls dismiss_notice() which writes to the site-wide DISMISSED_NOTICES option with no nonce verification and no current_user_can() check, allowing any authenticated user to permanently suppress admin notices for all users on the site.

detection_pattern.section_name: CSRF+AuthZ: AJAX dismiss handler without nonce or capability check
detection_pattern.description: Finds AJAX handlers whose action name contains 'dismiss'; these typically mutate persistent option state and should have both a nonce and a capability check. Files matching but not containing a nonce or capability verification are likely missing access controls.

findings.reachability: Any user who can submit a valid nonce for the target post (i.e., anyone who loaded the post-edit screen for that post ID, including contributors and authors on their own posts)
findings.justification: save_post verifies the nonce but never calls current_user_can('edit_post', $post_id), so any user who once loaded a post-edit screen for a given post and retained the nonce can set or delete the custom redirect URL on that post — including authors/contributors who lack the right to modify others' posts once they're published.

detection_pattern.section_name: AuthZ: save_post hook without capability check
detection_pattern.description: Finds save_post implementations that lack a current_user_can('edit_post') guard; a nonce alone is CSRF protection, not authorization — anyone with a valid nonce for any role can trigger the state change.

findings.reachability: Unauthenticated visitors — the redirect fires on every singular page request without any authentication gate
findings.justification: get_redirect() retrieves the raw URL value stored in post meta and passes it through absolute_url() which does not call esc_url() or wp_http_validate_url(), then hands the result to wp_redirect() with a 301 status; a stored URL such as 'javascript:alert(1)' (or any scheme) passes through unchallenged because clean_url() at save time only prepends 'http://' when the value starts with 'www.', and no scheme allowlist is enforced.

detection_pattern.section_name: Open redirect: wp_redirect without URL validation
detection_pattern.description: Matches wp_redirect calls where the URL argument is a variable; without esc_url or an allowlist check the destination could be an arbitrary or dangerous scheme stored earlier in the DB.

findings.reachability: Admin/editor who can edit posts — the script src is built by concatenating esc_url() output with an unescaped string literal suffix; however the suffix is a compile-time constant so this is not directly attacker-controlled. Risk is low but the pattern is fragile.
findings.justification: Line 592 emits '<script src="' . esc_url($this->get_url()) . 'dist/meta-box.js?v=' . self::CSS_JS_VERSION . '">'; the esc_url wraps only the plugin URL prefix, leaving the concatenated suffix outside the escaping call — if CSS_JS_VERSION were ever made dynamic or filterable, this becomes a script injection.

detection_pattern.section_name: Partial escaping: esc_url applied to only part of a concatenated URL
detection_pattern.description: Matches patterns where esc_url is applied then further string content is appended via concatenation outside the escaping call; the suffix is not sanitized.

findings.reachability: Any logged-in WordPress user; the handler is registered under wp_ajax_ (requires auth) but never verifies a nonce, so a CSRF request from a third-party page dismisses the notice for any logged-in admin
findings.justification: The plt_dismiss_notice AJAX handler reads $_GET['plt_notice'] and calls update_option() with no call to check_ajax_referer() or wp_verify_nonce(), making it trivially CSRF-able by any page that can lure the logged-in admin into making a GET request to admin-ajax.php?action=plt_dismiss_notice&plt_notice=4.

detection_pattern.section_name: CSRF: AJAX handler without nonce verification
detection_pattern.description: Finds all wp_ajax_ action registrations; the corresponding handler should contain check_ajax_referer or wp_verify_nonce. Handlers matched here but not the anti-pattern are missing CSRF protection.
